subject: HTML, CSS and JavaScript
topics: Object literals — what they are and how to create them
Object methods and how to call them
The 'this' keyword and why it is used
Arrays of objects — storing, looping, editing, deleting
What is the DOM and why it matters for dynamic webpages
DOM methods — getElementById, querySelector, querySelectorAll, innerHTML, innerText
DOM events — what they are and how they work
addEventListener and types of events — click, input, submit, keydown, mouseover
Reading and writing XML files in JavaScript
What is an XML file and why it is used

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using object literals in JavaScript?
options: To represent data structures with properties and methods
questions.explanation: Object literals provide a concise way to create objects, grouping related data (properties) and functions (methods) together.
questions.hint: Think about how you would represent a real-world entity like a 'car' in code.

questions.question: How do you access a property of an object named 'myObject' with the key 'name'?
questions.explanation: Dot notation (myObject.name) is the most common and readable way to access object properties.
questions.hint: Consider the standard way to access members of a structure in other programming languages.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'this' keyword within an object method?
options: It refers to the global object
It refers to the object that the method is called on
It refers to the function itself
It refers to the parent object
questions.explanation: 'this' provides a reference to the object context in which the method is being executed.
questions.hint: Imagine a method inside an object; 'this' helps it know which object it belongs to.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct way to add a new property to an existing JavaScript object?
questions.explanation: You can directly assign a value to a new property using dot notation or bracket notation.
questions.hint: Think about how you would assign a value to a variable.

questions.question: What happens when you try to access a non-existent property of an object?
options: The property is automatically created with a value of null
questions.explanation: Accessing a non-existent property returns 'undefined', indicating that the property has not been assigned a value.
questions.hint: Consider what a function would return if it couldn't find a requested value.

questions.question: How can you iterate over the properties of an object?
options: Using a for loop with index
questions.explanation: The 'for...in' loop is specifically designed to iterate over the enumerable properties of an object.
questions.hint: Think about loops designed for iterating over collections.

questions.question: What is the DOM?
options: A tree-like representation of the HTML document
questions.explanation: The Document Object Model (DOM) represents the structure of an HTML document as a tree, allowing JavaScript to interact with it.
questions.hint: Consider how a webpage is structured and how JavaScript needs to access its parts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'document' object in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The 'document' object is the root of the DOM tree and provides access to all elements within the HTML document.
questions.hint: Think about where you would start looking to find elements on a webpage.

questions.question: Which DOM method would you use to retrieve an element with a specific ID?
questions.explanation: 'getElementById()' is specifically designed to retrieve an element based on its unique ID attribute.
questions.hint: The name of the method suggests its purpose.

questions.question: What does 'querySelector()' return if no matching element is found?
questions.explanation: If 'querySelector()' doesn't find a matching element, it returns 'null'.
questions.hint: Consider what a search function would return if it couldn't find the requested item.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'innerHTML' and 'innerText'?
options: 'innerHTML' returns the text content, while 'innerText' returns the HTML content
'innerHTML' returns the HTML content, while 'innerText' returns the text content
They both return the same thing
They both return only text content
questions.explanation: 'innerHTML' gets or sets the HTML content of an element, while 'innerText' gets or sets only the visible text content.
questions.hint: Think about whether you want to include HTML tags in the returned value.

questions.question: What is an event in the context of the DOM?
options: A user interaction or browser event
questions.explanation: DOM events represent actions or occurrences that happen in the browser, such as clicks, key presses, or page loads.
questions.hint: Consider what triggers a change on a webpage.

questions.question: How do you attach an event listener to an HTML element?
questions.explanation: 'addEventListener()' is the standard method for attaching event listeners to elements, allowing multiple listeners for the same event.
questions.hint: The name of the method suggests its purpose.

questions.question: Which event is triggered when a form is submitted?
questions.explanation: The 'submit' event is fired when a form is submitted, typically by clicking a submit button.
questions.hint: Think about what happens when you press the 'Submit' button on a form.

questions.question: What is event bubbling?
options: Events only trigger on the target element
Events trigger on the target element and then propagate up the DOM tree
Events trigger on the parent element first
Events are cancelled after the first listener
questions.explanation: Event bubbling is the process where an event starts at the target element and then propagates up the DOM tree, triggering event listeners on parent elements.
questions.hint: Imagine a ripple effect starting from a point and expanding outwards.

questions.question: What is an XML file?
options: A markup language for storing and transporting data
questions.explanation: XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a markup language designed for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable.
questions.hint: Think about how data can be structured and transported between systems.

questions.question: Why would you use XML files in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: XML files are often used to store configuration data, exchange data between web servers and clients, and represent complex data structures.
questions.hint: Consider scenarios where you need to share data between different applications.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between HTML and XML?
options: HTML is stricter in its syntax than XML
XML is stricter in its syntax than HTML
HTML is designed for data storage, while XML is for display
XML is designed for data storage, while HTML is for display
questions.explanation: XML requires well-formed documents with proper nesting and closing tags, while HTML is more forgiving.
questions.hint: Think about the rules for creating valid documents in each language.

questions.question: Which JavaScript object is commonly used to parse XML data?
questions.explanation: The 'DOMParser' object is used to parse XML strings into a DOM tree, allowing you to access and manipulate the XML data.
questions.hint: The name of the object suggests its purpose.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'readyState' property in an XMLHttpRequest object?
options: It indicates the size of the XML file
It indicates the status of the request
It indicates the URL of the XML file
It indicates the type of XML file
questions.explanation: The 'readyState' property indicates the current state of the XMLHttpRequest, from uninitialized to completed.
questions.hint: Think about the different stages of making a request to a server.

questions.question: Consider the following object: `let person = {name: 'Alice', age: 30};`. How would you change Alice's age to 31?
questions.explanation: You can directly modify the value of an object property using dot notation or bracket notation.
questions.hint: Think about how you would update a variable's value.

questions.question: What will be the output of the following code? `let obj = {a: 1, b: 2}; console.log(obj.c);`
questions.explanation: Accessing a non-existent property of an object returns 'undefined'.
questions.hint: Consider what happens when you try to access a key that doesn't exist in an object.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using methods within an object?
options: They allow you to store data
They allow you to perform actions related to the object's data
They allow you to define the object's properties
They allow you to create new objects
questions.explanation: Methods encapsulate behavior related to the object, promoting code organization and reusability.
questions.hint: Think about how methods relate to the data they operate on.

questions.question: What is the output of the following code? `let obj = {x: 10}; let y = obj.x; y = 20; console.log(obj.x);`
questions.explanation: Changing the value of 'y' does not affect the value of 'x' in the original object, as 'y' holds a copy of the value.
questions.hint: Consider the difference between assigning a value and creating a reference.

questions.question: How would you create an array of objects, where each object represents a student with properties 'name' and 'grade'?
questions.explanation: You create an empty array and then push objects with the desired properties into it.
questions.hint: Think about how to store multiple items in a collection.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `querySelectorAll()` compared to `querySelector()`?
options: `querySelectorAll()` returns only the first matching element, while `querySelector()` returns all matching elements.
`querySelectorAll()` returns all matching elements, while `querySelector()` returns only the first matching element.
They both return the same result.
`querySelectorAll()` is used for IDs, while `querySelector()` is used for classes.
questions.explanation: `querySelector()` returns the first element that matches a specified CSS selector, while `querySelectorAll()` returns a NodeList of all matching elements.
questions.hint: Consider the 'All' in the name of one of the methods.

questions.question: What is the difference between `event.preventDefault()` and `event.stopPropagation()`?
options: `preventDefault()` stops the event from triggering the default action, while `stopPropagation()` prevents the event from bubbling up the DOM tree.
`stopPropagation()` stops the event from triggering the default action, while `preventDefault()` prevents the event from bubbling up the DOM tree.
They both do the same thing.
They both prevent the event from happening.
questions.explanation: `preventDefault()` prevents the browser's default behavior for an event (e.g., submitting a form), while `stopPropagation()` stops the event from propagating to parent elements.
questions.hint: Think about controlling the default behavior of an event versus controlling its path through the DOM.

questions.question: Which event listener is best suited for handling real-time changes in an input field?
questions.explanation: The 'input' event is fired whenever the value of an input field changes, making it ideal for real-time updates.
questions.hint: Consider which event is triggered with every keystroke or change in the input field.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `onload` event in an `XMLHttpRequest` object?
options: It is triggered when the request is initialized.
It is triggered when the request fails.
It is triggered when the request completes successfully.
It is triggered when the request is aborted.
questions.explanation: The `onload` event is triggered when the `XMLHttpRequest` has successfully completed and the response is available.
questions.hint: Think about when you would want to process the data received from the server.

questions.question: What is the potential security risk when directly manipulating the DOM with user-provided data?
questions.explanation: Directly inserting user-provided data into the DOM without proper sanitization can lead to XSS attacks, where malicious scripts are executed in the user's browser.
questions.hint: Consider how attackers might inject malicious code through user input.

questions.question: Consider this code: `let obj = {a: 1}; let newObj = obj; newObj.a = 2; console.log(obj.a);`. What will be the output?
questions.explanation: Because `newObj` is assigned a reference to `obj`, modifying `newObj.a` also modifies `obj.a`.
questions.hint: Think about the difference between copying a value and creating a reference.

questions.question: What is the best practice for handling errors when parsing XML data in JavaScript?
options: Ignore the errors and continue processing.
Use a try...catch block to handle potential parsing errors.
Rely on the browser's default error handling.
Log the errors to the console and refresh the page.
questions.explanation: Using a `try...catch` block allows you to gracefully handle parsing errors and prevent the script from crashing.
questions.hint: Consider how to prevent unexpected errors from disrupting your application.

questions.question: Which DOM method is most efficient for adding a new element to the end of a list?
questions.explanation: `appendChild()` is specifically designed for adding a new child element to the end of a parent element, making it the most efficient option.
questions.hint: Think about the purpose of each method and which one directly addresses the task.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using 'use strict' in JavaScript?
options: To enforce stricter parsing and error handling
questions.explanation: 'use strict' enables stricter parsing and error handling, helping to catch common coding mistakes and improve code quality.
questions.hint: Consider how to write more robust and maintainable code.

questions.question: What is the difference between `==` and `===` in JavaScript?
options: `==` compares values and types, while `===` compares only values.
`===` compares values and types, while `==` compares only values.
`==` is used for numbers, while `===` is used for strings.
questions.explanation: `===` (strict equality) compares both the value and the type, while `==` (loose equality) performs type coercion before comparing values.
questions.hint: Consider the importance of data types in comparisons.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `defer` attribute in a `<script>` tag?
options: To execute the script after the page has loaded
questions.explanation: The `defer` attribute tells the browser to download the script in parallel with parsing the HTML, but to execute it only after the HTML parsing is complete.
questions.hint: Think about how to improve page loading performance.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using CSS classes instead of inline styles?
options: Inline styles are easier to maintain
CSS classes allow for better separation of concerns and reusability
CSS classes are only for simple styling
questions.explanation: CSS classes promote separation of concerns, allowing you to define styles in a central location and apply them to multiple elements, improving maintainability and reusability.
questions.hint: Consider the principles of good web development practices.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `bind()` method in JavaScript?
options: To set the 'this' value for a function
To convert a function to a string
questions.explanation: The `bind()` method creates a new function that, when called, has its 'this' keyword set to the provided value.
questions.hint: Think about controlling the context in which a function is executed.

questions.question: What is the difference between `localStorage` and `sessionStorage`?
options: `localStorage` stores data for one session, while `sessionStorage` stores data permanently.
`sessionStorage` stores data for one session, while `localStorage` stores data permanently.
They both store data for one session.
questions.explanation: `sessionStorage` stores data for only one session (until the browser tab or window is closed), while `localStorage` stores data permanently (until explicitly deleted).
questions.hint: Consider the lifespan of the data you want to store.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `map()` method on arrays?
options: To filter elements from an array
To reduce an array to a single value
To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
questions.explanation: The `map()` method transforms each element of an array and returns a new array with the transformed values.
questions.hint: Think about how to apply a function to every item in a collection.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `reduce()` method on arrays?
options: To filter elements from an array
To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
questions.explanation: The `reduce()` method applies a function against an accumulator and each element in the array (from left to right) to reduce it to a single value.
questions.hint: Think about how to combine all elements of an array into a single result.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `filter()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To create a new array with all elements that pass the test implemented by the provided function
questions.explanation: The `filter()` method creates a new array with all elements that pass the test implemented by the provided function.
questions.hint: Think about how to select specific elements from an array based on a condition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `forEach()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To execute a provided function once for each array element
questions.explanation: The `forEach()` method executes a provided function once for each array element.
questions.hint: Think about how to perform an action on each element of an array.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `find()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To return the first element in the array that satisfies the provided testing function
questions.explanation: The `find()` method returns the first element in the array that satisfies the provided testing function.
questions.hint: Think about how to search for a specific element in an array.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `some()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To test whether at least one element in the array passes the test implemented by the provided function
questions.explanation: The `some()` method tests whether at least one element in the array passes the test implemented by the provided function.
questions.hint: Think about how to check if any element in an array meets a certain condition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `every()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To test whether all elements in the array pass the test implemented by the provided function
questions.explanation: The `every()` method tests whether all elements in the array pass the test implemented by the provided function.
questions.hint: Think about how to check if all elements in an array meet a certain condition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `sort()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To sort the elements of an array in place
To filter elements from an array
questions.explanation: The `sort()` method sorts the elements of an array in place (modifies the original array).
questions.hint: Think about how to arrange elements in a specific order.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `splice()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To add or remove elements from an array
To filter elements from an array
questions.explanation: The `splice()` method changes the contents of an array by removing or replacing existing elements and/or adding new elements in place.
questions.hint: Think about how to modify an array by adding or removing elements.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `slice()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To extract a section of an array and return it as a new array
To filter elements from an array
questions.explanation: The `slice()` method returns a shallow copy of a portion of an array into a new array object.
questions.hint: Think about how to create a subset of an array without modifying the original.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `concat()` method on arrays?
options: To create a new array with the results of calling a provided function on every element in the original array
To reduce an array to a single value
To merge two or more arrays and return a new array
To filter elements from an array
questions.explanation: The `concat()` method is used to merge two or more arrays. This method does not change the existing arrays, but instead returns a new array.
questions.hint: Think about how to combine multiple arrays into a single array.
